claims.text: In October 1957, three Royal Navy cadets — William Laing, Michael Crowley, and Ray Baker — descended a hill into Kersey, Suffolk and walked into a deathly silent, summer-green ghost-village whose air was 'dead', whose smoke was frozen mid-rise, and whose butcher shop displayed three rotting oxen carcasses but no flies.

evidence.quote:
Three Royal Navy cadets walked across
green hills on a training exercise, map
reading and orientation. William Lang,
Michael Crowley, and Ray Baker, a
beautiful Sunday morning. Birds sang,
church bells rang in the distan

oxen carcasses with flesh rotting off
the bone.
[Music]
They weren't the carcasses of cows. They
were oxen. Oxen were used as work
animals, not for meat. Nobody in England
slaughtered oxen anymore. The carcasses
hung by

claims.rationale: The Kersey time-slip is a real and famous case in British paranormal literature, described in Andrew MacKenzie's book 'Adventures in Time' (1997) based on William Laing's correspondence. Whether the cadets actually had a paranormal experience or constructed/embellished the memory decades later is unverifiable — only Laing's later testimony exists, and his two companions confirmed less detail. The story exists; its supernatural interpretation is a single-source claim.

claims.text: When the cadets fled back up the hill, the village instantly became normal — cars, dispersing smoke, ringing bells, completed church tower — proving (in the host's framing) that what they had seen was not a delusion but a discrete temporal anomaly that ended sharply.

evidence.quote:
looked back at
Kersey.
It looked normal. A car drove down the
main street. Smoke dispersed naturally
in the wind. The church bell rang out,
clear and normal. The church tower
wasn't mid-construction, it stood tall
agains

claims.rationale: This is the dramatic punctuation MacKenzie's account adds to the case. The cleaner version of events is more rhetorically effective than evidentially robust. There is no contemporaneous corroboration — no Sunday-1957 photograph, no third party who saw a 'normal' Kersey from the same vantage point. Discrete on/off framing is part of how time-slip narratives are constructed in the literature.

claims.text: Andrew MacKenzie's archival research showed Kersey's 'butcher shop' building was constructed in 1350 (the year the Black Death reached the village), and the cadets had seen Kersey roughly as it appeared around 1420 — a recovering plague-haunted village, with the trauma still 'imprinted' on the place six centuries later.

evidence.quote:
building was built in 1350. Records
showed it operated as a butcher shop for
over a century. Lang's face went pale.
1350 was the year of Black Death reached
Courcey.
The plague killed half of England.
People died faster

1420, 70 years after the plague. A
village that survived. But the quiet
wasn't gone. The trauma of 1350 burned
so deeply into Courcey that it could
still be experienced six centuries
later. The land remembered the plague

claims.rationale: Kersey is genuinely a medieval village; many of its timber-framed buildings date to the 14th-15th centuries; the Black Death did devastate Suffolk in 1349-50. Those underlying historical facts are correct. The interpretive leap that Laing's experience was a 'memory of place' from circa 1420 is MacKenzie's narrative reconstruction, not an empirical finding. Place-memory / 'stone tape' theory has no scientific support.

dependencies.rationale: provides the historical interpretation MacKenzie applied to the cadets' experience

claims.text: In February 1983, Pennsylvania prison inmate Don Decker — on furlough for his abusive grandfather's funeral — was followed by water that materialized out of mid-air around him, first at his friends' home and then inside his concrete jail cell, soaking observers but leaving ceilings and walls dry.

evidence.quote:
Decker stood in the bathroom of his
friend's house, washing his hands in the
sink. The temperature dropped 30° in a
second. He could see his breath.
Then he felt a drop of water hit the top
of his head. He looked up at t

Solid concrete, no
pipes in the ceiling, no windows to the
outside. The sounds of the other
prisoners suddenly faded away. The air
turned cold. A mist formed near the
ceiling. Don looked up. A drop of water
hit his face,

claims.rationale: The Don Decker 'rain man' case is genuinely on record: it was investigated by Stroudsburg police, featured on Unsolved Mysteries (1993), and multiple corrections officers (Walbert, Keenhold) gave on-camera accounts repeating the host's description. As the host says, the witnesses had no obvious motive to lie. However, mass mis-perception, plumbing leaks behind walls, and condensation cycles in concrete cells could account for some/most of the reported events. The case is real; the supernatural reading is one possible explanation.

claims.text: Officer Walbert physically inspected the ceiling of Don's cell and confirmed it was dry while water continued to fall on Don and on his own uniform — a direct sensory verification that ruled out plumbing as the source.

evidence.quote:
checked the ceiling with his hands. Bone
dry. He checked the walls. Also dry. The
water existed only in the air around
Don.
Now, Walbert was a straight arrow. He
dealt in facts, evidence, things that
could be measured an

claims.rationale: Walbert's testimony is the strongest single piece of evidence in the case and was filmed for the 1993 Unsolved Mysteries segment. He is a real, named, identifiable witness who has consistently maintained his account for decades and had no obvious motive to fabricate. This raises the credibility of the case substantially above pure folklore but does not by itself establish the paranormal — single-witness perception under stress can still err.

claims.text: When ordered by Lieutenant Keenhold to demonstrate his alleged power, Don made a single drop of water hit the lieutenant's desk and then was thrown across the room by an invisible force, after which three parallel claw-mark scratches opened spontaneously on his neck and cheeks while six officers watched.

evidence.quote:
make it rain in here, right now.
Don looked at him with a blank
expression. He didn't argue or protest.
He concentrated. Then the room was
silent. The fluorescent lights
flickered, and a single drop of water
hit the lieu

Three deep scratches appeared on his
neck, claw marks, as if something
invisible raked him with sharp fingers.
The officers watched the skin split open
in real time. Blood welled up and ran
down his collar. There was no

claims.rationale: The 'on demand' demonstration in the lieutenant's office is part of Keenhold's recorded testimony but is a step further than the dry-ceiling/wet-uniform observations and brings in extra phenomena (telekinetic throws, spontaneous wounding) that are far harder to verify. Self-inflicted scratches in dissociative states are well-documented in psychiatric literature, providing a mundane alternative explanation for the cuts.

claims.text: The Don Decker phenomena are an example of recurrent spontaneous psychokinesis (RSPK), a documented pattern in which extreme repressed trauma — in Don's case, a lifetime of unprocessed childhood abuse — appears to manifest as physical environmental disturbances around the affected person.

evidence.quote:
recurrent spontaneous psychokinesis,
RSPK. It's been documented over 100
times. Some people who go through
extreme trauma experience physical
effects. Don had a lifetime of rage
locked inside him, years of abuse with
no

claims.rationale: RSPK is a real category of parapsychology research, dating from William Roll's work on poltergeist agents in the 1960s-70s. The 'troubled adolescent or trauma survivor at the center' pattern is a real recurring feature of poltergeist case files. Mainstream psychology, however, generally explains these cases as combinations of fraud (especially by the central person), mass suggestion, mundane physics, and selective reporting. RSPK as a phenomenon is well-defined; as a real causal mechanism it remains unproven.

claims.text: On April 5, 2015 a YouTube channel called 'Unfavorable Semicircle' appeared and uploaded 1,247 videos in its first 24 hours, eventually reaching three uploads per minute and over 72,000 videos within 9 months — a rate that should have been impossible under YouTube's then-existing per-account upload limits.

evidence.quote:
April 5th, 2015, a
YouTube channel appeared with a nonsense
name, Unfavorable Semicircle. That first
day, that channel uploaded 1,247
videos within 24 hours. The first was
titled The Sagittarius Symbol, followed
by 98070

72,000 videos.
No human could do that.
The internet noticed. They thought it
was a bot, or a glitch. Then they
started combining the videos. Thousands
of frames assembled into composites. An
image appeared.
A man in what

claims.rationale: Unfavorable Semicircle is a real and well-documented YouTube oddity that ran 2015-2017. The April 5, 2015 first-upload date and the eventual 70k+ video count are accurate per Reddit-archived data and the 2016 BBC article. The 'three uploads per minute' framing is approximately correct as a peak rate. Solid factual claim.

claims.text: When Reddit investigators identified specific patterns in Unfavorable Semicircle's content — particular symbols, naming conventions — the channel demonstrably altered its uploads in real time to abandon those patterns, suggesting an intelligence (human or otherwise) was monitoring and adapting to the investigation.

evidence.quote:
channel had almost no subscribers.
It wasn't trying to go viral. It was
broadcasting into the void and whoever
was supposed to receive the message
already knew where to look.
But then something unexpected happened.
The c

claims.rationale: Some of the Reddit investigation logs do show pattern shifts in the channel after specific posts, which the host frames as adaptive response. The simpler explanation is that the original creator was an active human who read the same Reddit threads and adjusted output to maintain mystique. That is consistent with a 2022 'BrickSails' Twitter claim of authorship. 'Real-time adaptive intelligence' is consistent with both supernatural and very mundane authorship.

claims.text: When 'lock' video frames in Unfavorable Semicircle's second wave were assembled side by side, they composited into a recognizable image of NASA's 1977 Voyager Golden Record — implying the channel was in some sense 'replying' to humanity's interstellar message.

evidence.quote:
Voyager
Golden Record, the message humanity sent
to aliens in 1977.
Unfavorable Semicircle was responding to
our message to the stars.
The final broadcast came without
warning, encoded in a garbled post on
the channel's

claims.rationale: Reddit investigators did produce a composite that was identified as resembling the Voyager Record's plaque imagery. As ARG-style or art-project content, this is a clever flourish; as evidence of an 'extraterrestrial reply' it is one composite produced by motivated pattern-seekers. Pareidolia in large image-set composites is common.

claims.text: Coordinates encoded in Unfavorable Semicircle's final Google Plus post resolved to an empty patch of mid-Atlantic Ocean where 1982 sonar data from a research vessel logged a 130-foot metallic object the Navy subsequently classified.

evidence.quote:
sonar data,
something's down there, 130 ft long and
metallic.
The sonar reading dated back to 1982, a
research vessel conducting deep ocean
mapping. They logged the object, ran
multiple passes to confirm. The
signature d

claims.rationale: I can find no public reference outside Why-Files-style retellings to a 1982 sonar contact at coordinates allegedly broadcast by Unfavorable Semicircle. The Navy classifies many sonar contacts; no specific declassified report ties a 130-foot mid-Atlantic metallic anomaly to this story. This is the most evidentially weak claim in the segment and feels like creative narrative embellishment.

claims.text: Unfavorable Semicircle's upload pathway bypassed YouTube's normal verification and compression and traced to a data center in Iceland whose equipment had been offline for months — a pathway YouTube had to actively close after the ban, and which mysteriously reopened in 2022 for a 48-hour copycat.

evidence.quote:
data center in Iceland. The equipment
was offline and had been for months.
Now, after the channel disappeared,
copycat accounts appeared, dozens of
them, reposting the original videos.
Some claimed to be archivists. Some

claims.rationale: I cannot find independent corroboration that 'Engineers' (publicly identifiable ones) said the channel bypassed YouTube's normal pipeline, or that the trace led to offline equipment in Iceland. This is the kind of detail that floats between paranormal aggregator sites without primary sourcing. The basic fact that the channel achieved unusually high upload rates is established; the specific Iceland-data-center detail is unverified.

claims.text: These three stories — the Kersey time-slip, Don Decker's water phenomena, and Unfavorable Semicircle — are presented as 'campfire stories that are true and unsolved' rather than debunked, with the host explicitly noting this episode contains no analysis or skepticism.

evidence.quote:
AJ. This is The Why Files and
that was a campfire story. No debunking,
no analysis, just a creepy story to
scare you and the kids. And that one is
true and unsolved. If you had fun, I'd
appreciate if you can like, subscribe

claims.rationale: The host explicitly states this in the closing remarks, which is exactly what makes this a campfire-format episode rather than the usual Why-Files investigate-then-debunk structure. This is a meta-claim about the episode itself and is straightforwardly true.
